categoryName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: This schematic illustrates the embryologic origin of a pancreatic anomaly that can encircle and obstruct the second part of the duodenum. Which developmental error produces it?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Abnormal rotation of the ventral pancreatic bud so it surrounds the duodenum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failure of the dorsal pancreatic bud to form

choices.label: D
choices.text: Failure of neural crest cell migration

explanation.summary: The pancreas forms from ventral and dorsal buds. If the ventral bud rotates abnormally, it can partially or completely encircle the duodenum (annular pancreas), sometimes causing duodenal obstruction with bilious vomiting and a double-bubble appearance.
explanation.whyCorrect: The pancreas forms from ventral and dorsal buds. If the ventral bud rotates abnormally, it can partially or completely encircle the duodenum (annular pancreas), sometimes causing duodenal obstruction with bilious vomiting and a double-bubble appearance.

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: The right-hand panel of this diagram shows small bowel twisted around its vascular pedicle. Which underlying anatomic abnormality predisposes an infant to this complication?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A narrow mesenteric base from failed 270-degree midgut rotation

explanation.summary: When the midgut fails to complete its normal 270-degree counterclockwise rotation, the mesentery anchors over a very short base rather than a broad one. This narrow pedicle allows the bowel to twist on itself (volvulus), compromising the superior mesenteric vessels and risking necrosis of the entire small intestine.
explanation.whyCorrect: When the midgut fails to complete its normal 270-degree counterclockwise rotation, the mesentery anchors over a very short base rather than a broad one. This narrow pedicle allows the bowel to twist on itself (volvulus), compromising the superior mesenteric vessels and risking necrosis of the entire small intestine.

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: These illustrations depict variants in which the rectum connects abnormally to the urinary or genital tract. Faulty development of which septum accounts for this entire group of anomalies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The transverse septum of the septum transversum

explanation.summary: The urorectal septum divides the cloaca into anorectal and urogenital portions. Abnormal development leaves fistulous connections between the rectum and the urethra, bladder, or vestibule (and, at the extreme, a persistent cloaca), as illustrated across these variants.
explanation.whyCorrect: The urorectal septum divides the cloaca into anorectal and urogenital portions. Abnormal development leaves fistulous connections between the rectum and the urethra, bladder, or vestibule (and, at the extreme, a persistent cloaca), as illustrated across these variants.

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: These ultrasound images compare a normal pylorus (left) with an abnormal one (right, circled). Which sonographic feature confirms the diagnosis on the right?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased pyloric muscle wall thickness and an elongated pyloric channel

choices.label: B
choices.text: A target/donut sign of telescoped bowel

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bird-beak narrowing of the distal esophagus

choices.label: E
choices.text: A dilated aganglionic megacolon with transition zone

explanation.summary: Ultrasound is the confirmatory test for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, revealing an enlarged pylorus with increased muscle wall thickness (roughly >3 mm) and elongated channel length compared with the thin, short normal pylorus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ultrasound is the confirmatory test for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, revealing an enlarged pylorus with increased muscle wall thickness (roughly >3 mm) and elongated channel length compared with the thin, short normal pylorus.

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: This upper GI contrast study of an infant shows a 'corkscrew' proximal small bowel that fails to cross to the left of the vertebral column. This finding confirms which diagnosis?

explanation.summary: In malrotation the duodenum lacks a normal C-loop and does not cross the midline; when volvulus occurs the proximal jejunum shows a classic corkscrew pattern. An upper GI study is the confirmatory test in a stable infant with bilious vomiting.
explanation.whyCorrect: In malrotation the duodenum lacks a normal C-loop and does not cross the midline; when volvulus occurs the proximal jejunum shows a classic corkscrew pattern. An upper GI study is the confirmatory test in a stable infant with bilious vomiting.

questions.topicName: Congenital Disorders of the Gastrointestinal Tract
questions.stem: This contrast enema shows a narrowed distal segment with an abrupt transition to markedly dilated proximal colon. What is the underlying cause of this appearance?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A mesenteric vascular accident with segmental resorption

choices.label: D
choices.text: Twisting of bowel around a narrow mesentery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Absence of ganglion cells from failed neural crest migration in the distal bowel

explanation.summary: In Hirschsprung disease, neural crest cells fail to migrate into the distal gut, leaving an aganglionic, tonically contracted segment (usually rectosigmoid). The narrowed aganglionic bowel cannot relax, so the normally innervated proximal colon dilates, creating the transition zone seen on contrast enema.
explanation.whyCorrect: In Hirschsprung disease, neural crest cells fail to migrate into the distal gut, leaving an aganglionic, tonically contracted segment (usually rectosigmoid). The narrowed aganglionic bowel cannot relax, so the normally innervated proximal colon dilates, creating the transition zone seen on contrast enema.
